title: Leaders and followers
paperName: Paper 3 - Specialist Options: Approaches, Issues and Debates
summary: 9990 Organisational — leader-member exchange, followership, and power in organisations.
learningObjectives: Analyse types of followership and engagement.
Evaluate French & Raven's bases of power.
Assess two-way nature of leadership relationships.

simpleExplanation.title: Leadership is a relationship
simpleExplanation.summary: Leaders and followers co-create outcomes — in-groups get more trust and resources, while power flows from expertise, position, and charisma.
steps: Power bases — reward, coercive, legitimate, expert, referent.

flashcards.back: Leaders develop different quality relationships — in-group gets trust, autonomy, and information; out-group gets formal contract only.
Alienated, conformist, pragmatist, passive, exemplary (star) — based on independent thinking vs active engagement.
Legitimate, reward, coercive, expert, referent — later added informational power.
Influence through admiration and identification — followers want to be like the leader.
Influence from knowledge and skill — strongest for technical decisions.
Leadership requires willing followers; passive or alienated followers undermine even skilled leaders.

sections.content: **Leaders and followers** (4.2.3) completes 4.2. Don't ignore **followership** in essays — leadership is co-produced, not one-directional.

sections.question: A project manager gives two trusted engineers early access to plans and flexible deadlines, while contractors receive only standard briefs and close monitoring. Contractors complain of unfair treatment. Analyse using LMX and French & Raven.
sections.solution:
**LMX:** Engineers are **in-group** — high **exchange quality**, mutual trust, informal influence. Contractors are **out-group** — transactional, low discretion. This predicts higher **commitment and performance** from in-group but **resentment and turnover risk** from out-group.

**French & Raven:** Manager uses **legitimate** (formal role) and **coercive** (monitoring threat) power over contractors; **reward** and **referent** power with engineers who identify with the manager's vision.

**Followership:** Engineers show **exemplary followership** (critical thinking + engagement); some contractors may become **alienated** (low engagement, high criticism).

**Evaluation:** LMX explains favouritism's effects but **does not justify** unfairness — organisations need **procedural justice**. High LMX for a few can harm **team cohesion** (see 4.3.3). **Expert power** should extend to any skilled contractor, not only favourites.

sections.label: Mark a leaders and followers question
